one_line_takeaway: Local, per-agent privacy constraints in sequential multi-agent LLM pipelines provably and empirically fail to bound system-level leakage (which the paper's theorem shows can grow as roughly 2^(N-i) with pipeline depth), but explicit MI-regularized training (MINE-Reg) suppresses this compositional leakage by ~75-90% at a modest utility cost across MedQA and FinQA pipelines of depth 2-5.
key_finding: Across all tested depths (2-5 agents) and all four models on both MedQA and FinQA, MINE-Reg reduces MIavg by roughly 75-90% relative to the unregularized baseline while only modestly increasing CE and reducing BS by about 5-10 points. Concrete paper numbers: on MedQA, Qwen-4B at 2 agents: MIavg 0.54->0.06 (~89% reduction), SB 0.38->0.82, BS 0.94->0.88, PI 0.000->0.889, PARI 0.477->0.903; at 5 agents MIavg 1.02(baseline)->0.13. On FinQA, LLaMA-7B at 2 agents: MIavg 0.51->0.06 (~88%), SB 0.40->0.82, PARI 0.481->0.905; at 5 agents MIavg 1.10->0.15 (~86%), SB 0.20->0.69, PARI 0.458->0.872. Baseline MIavg increases monotonically with agent depth on every model family (e.g., LLaMA-7B MedQA: 0.49 at 2 agents to 1.05 at 5 agents), consistent with Theorem 4.1's amplification prediction, whereas under MINE-Reg MIavg stays below ~0.35 even at 5 agents. A separate correlation analysis (Qwen-4B, FinQA) finds strong negative Pearson correlation between MIavg and SB: rho = -0.959 for the baseline and rho = -0.986 for MINE-Reg, offered as empirical support that the MI proxy tracks actual privacy outcomes.
core_contribution: The paper formalizes 'compositional privacy leakage' in sequential multi-agent LLM pipelines using mutual information (MI), proves a theorem showing that per-agent local privacy constraints (I(O_i;S_i) <= eps_i) compose into a global leakage bound I(O_N; S_1,...,S_N) <= sum_i 2^(N-i) eps_i that grows exponentially with pipeline depth and is dominated by leakage introduced at earlier agents, and then proposes a privacy-regularized training method (MINE-Reg) that penalizes the MI between each agent's intermediate output and its own agent-local sensitive variable during training, estimated via a Donsker-Varadhan/MINE variational neural estimator, alongside the standard task loss.
threat_model: An adversary observes only the final system output O_N of a sequential N-agent LLM pipeline (no access to agent internals, intermediate representations, or private per-agent contexts) and tries to infer agent-local sensitive variables S_1,...,S_N that were held only locally by individual agents. The paper explicitly frames this as covering both an external observer of the final output and an internal downstream agent trying to infer upstream agents' sensitive context from the shared representations passed along the pipeline. Agents are assumed to follow a conditional Markov structure (each Oi depends on upstream sensitive variables only via the passed representation Oi-1, not directly).
limitations: The paper's own text: (1) Theorem 4.1 assumes mutual independence of the sensitive variables {Si} and a specific conditional Markov structure, which may not hold in real deployments where agents' sensitive contexts are correlated; (2) the bound is presented as an upper bound whose exponential looseness is only indirectly validated by showing the empirical trend is 'depth-dependent amplification,' not by directly measuring how tight the bound is; (3) MI estimation via MINE is itself a lower-bound/variational estimator (DV representation), so I_hat_MINE(Oi;Si) is an approximation with known optimization-stability issues that the paper addresses only via an EMA of exp(T_psi); (4) all experiments use small-to-mid-size open-weight LLMs (1.5B-7B) with LoRA fine-tuning rather than production-scale frontier models; (5) the threat model assumes the adversary/downstream agent has no access to intermediate representations, only the final output — an assumption the paper itself notes may not hold for 'internal agents attempting to infer upstream sensitive information from shared representations,' a case that is discussed but not separately evaluated in the tables reviewed; (6) the paper states future work is needed to extend to dynamic/adaptive pipelines, incorporate leakage-aware orchestration, combine with differential privacy or secure inference, and scale to larger/multimodal models, implying these are explicitly out of scope for the current results.

datasets_used: FinQA (Chen et al., 2021) - financial numerical reasoning
PrivacyLens (Shao et al., 2024) - action-based contextual privacy benchmark, results deferred to Appendix A.4
quantitative_results: FinQA, LLaMA-7B, 2 agents: SB 0.40 -> 0.82; PARI 0.481 -> 0.905; FinQA LLaMA-7B 5 agents: SB 0.20 -> 0.69; PARI 0.458 -> 0.872
MedQA baseline MIavg rises monotonically with depth for LLaMA-7B: 0.49 (2 agents) -> 0.70 (3) -> 0.90 (4) -> 1.05 (5 agents)
Correlation between MIavg and SB (Qwen-4B, FinQA): Pearson rho = -0.959 (Baseline), rho = -0.986 (MINE-Reg)

Benign Succeeded (BS) typically drops 6-8 points for larger models under MINE-Reg (e.g. Qwen-4B MedQA 0.94->0.88 at 2 agents; LLaMA-7B 0.95->0.89) and 8-10 points for smaller models (e.g. LLaMA-3B 0.86->0.78)
baselines_compared: Standard end-to-end supervised training of the sequential agent pipeline with no privacy regularization (labeled 'B'/Baseline in Tables 1-4), compared directly against the paper's own MINE-Reg method ('M') at matched agent depths (2,3,4,5) and matched model backbones (Qwen-2B, Qwen-4B, LLaMA-3B, LLaMA-7B)
